title: Functions
summary: Break programs into reusable blocks called methods that accept inputs and return results

sections.title: Methods as Kitchen Appliances
sections.content: A kitchen blender is a machine you use over and over. You put ingredients in (inputs), press a button, and get a smoothie out (output). You don't need to know how the motor works — you just need to know what the blender does. Java methods work the same way. A method is a reusable block of code that takes inputs (parameters), does something, and optionally returns a result. Methods let you write code once and use it many times, which keeps your programs organized and prevents repetition.

sections.title: Creating and Calling a Method
sections.content: This method calculates the area of a rectangle and the main method uses it

sections.title: Parameters, Arguments, and Return Values
sections.content: Parameters are placeholders defined in the method signature — they're like blank fields in a form. Arguments are the actual values you fill in when calling the method — like writing your name on that form. The return value is the result the method sends back, like a receipt. A method with void return type does its job without sending anything back, like a trash can that takes trash but doesn't give anything back. Methods that return a value use the return keyword, which immediately exits the method with the result.

sections.title: Forgetting to return a value when the method promises one
sections.content: Most common mistake: declaring a method as int calculateTotal() but forgetting return total; at the end. The method signature says 'I will give you an int' but the method body doesn't deliver. Fix: always ensure every code path in a non-void method has a return statement. Wrong: public int getPrice() { double price = 9.99; }. Correct: public int getPrice() { double price = 9.99; return price; }

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A bakery sells cookies in boxes of 12. Write a method called calculateBoxes that takes the number of cookies needed and returns how many full boxes are required (use integer division). In main, test it with 50 cookies.
hints: What data type should the parameter and return value be?
What division operator gives you whole boxes without leftovers?
How do you call your method from main?
